skill.name: security-checklist
skill.description: Security review guidance for web applications, using OWASP Top 10 controls, authentication patterns, input validation, dependency scanning, and compliance checks.
skill.summary: Audit web application security with OWASP checklists and practical review steps.
skill.author: AI Agent Hub

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many dangerous patterns, but review shows they are mostly documentation examples, defensive test payloads, and checklist items. No malicious intent or prompt injection was found. The skill still carries medium operational risk because it instructs agents to run local scanners, write scan reports, and handle secret-scan results.

medium_findings.title: Local Security Tool Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for operational risk. The skill instructs agents to run npm audit, pip-audit, Semgrep, Bandit, TruffleHog, Trivy, and related shell commands. These are legitimate defensive tools, but they execute local commands, can create report files, and may contact package or vulnerability services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly shown and the surrounding text tells agents when to run them. The intent is defensive scanning, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Project-Mutating Dependency Fix Command
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for change risk. The skill includes npm audit fix as a recommended mitigation command. This command can modify dependency lockfiles or installed packages, so it should require user approval in managed workflows.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The mutating command is present in explicit remediation examples. It is common defensive guidance, but it can change a project state.

medium_findings.title: Secret Scan Output Handling
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for sensitive-data handling risk. The skill teaches agents to run secret scanners and save results, which may contain secret types or sensitive repository evidence. It does not direct exfiltration, but outputs should be treated as confidential.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The secret-scanning workflow and output file are explicit. The finding is moderate because the workflow is defensive and no external destination is provided for secrets.

low_findings.title: Dangerous Payloads Used as Defensive Test Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for malicious behavior. SQL injection strings, command substitution strings, internal IP addresses, and metadata endpoint URLs appear as security test cases and SSRF checklist items. They are not executed by the skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding headings are injection and SSRF testing sections. The payloads are checklist examples, not active code.

low_findings.title: Unsafe Code Patterns Presented as Anti-Patterns
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for direct exploit code. eval, exec, shell=True, weak hashing, and vulnerable request examples are presented as bad examples with safer alternatives or mitigation guidance. The documentation warns users to avoid these patterns.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code blocks label these patterns as bad or explicitly tell readers to avoid them. This strongly indicates instructional defensive context.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Keyword Matches Without Credential Access
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for credential theft. SECRET_KEY, environment variable guidance, private key mentions, and cookie SameSite text are part of secure configuration examples or checklist language. No evidence found of reading real secrets or sending them to a remote endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious keywords are in examples and checklist items. I found no code path that accesses actual environment secrets or performs exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Commands in Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes many shell command examples for security scanning. They are documented procedures, but agents should ask before running commands that install tools, scan repositories, or modify dependencies.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present in fenced documentation blocks. The risk depends on agent execution policy, not hidden code behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network and SSRF Test Targets
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill references localhost, private network ranges, metadata endpoints, CDN URLs, and HTTP client examples. These references support SSRF education and testing, not unauthorized access.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The context is explicitly an SSRF section with validation and blocking guidance. The examples are not standalone execution instructions.

content.user_title: Audit Application Security with OWASP Checklists
content.value_statement: Security reviews often miss common web risks and inconsistent evidence collection. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured OWASP guidance, scanner workflows, and review checklists.
actual_capabilities: Guide OWASP Top 10 reviews for access control, injection, SSRF, logging, and configuration risks.
Recommend secure authentication, password hashing, session, JWT, and MFA patterns.
Provide dependency and static analysis workflows for npm audit, pip-audit, Semgrep, Bandit, TruffleHog, and Trivy.
Help record security scan evidence, severity counts, and release blocking decisions.
Map security checks to GDPR and SOC 2 control areas.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not prove an application is secure.
Scanner commands may require installed tools, network access, or user approval.
Automated scan results can include false positives and need human review.
Compliance checklists do not replace legal, audit, or certification advice.

use_cases.title: Review a Web Feature Before Release
use_cases.description: Check authentication, authorization, input validation, logging, dependency risks, and security headers before deployment.

use_cases.title: Prepare Evidence for a Security Review
use_cases.description: Collect scan results, severity counts, and mitigation notes in a consistent format for reviewers.

use_cases.title: Harden an Existing Application
use_cases.description: Use OWASP controls to find gaps in sessions, secrets, SSRF protection, dependency hygiene, and monitoring.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Security Checklist
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small application or feature.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the security-checklist skill to review this feature against the OWASP Top 10. List the highest risks, missing controls, and simple fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Authentication Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Focused review of login, sessions, and authorization.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the security-checklist skill to assess our authentication and authorization design. Cover password storage, MFA, sessions, JWTs, rate limits, and access control.

prompt_templates.title: Release Readiness Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the security-checklist skill to prepare a release security review. Include scanner commands to run, evidence to collect, blocking thresholds, and remediation priorities.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Threat Model Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Deep review for a sensitive production system.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the security-checklist skill to threat model this architecture. Evaluate OWASP risks, SSRF boundaries, secret handling, supply chain controls, logging, monitoring, and compliance gaps.

output_examples.input: Review a new password reset flow before release.
output: High priority: require generic responses for valid and invalid email addresses.
Add rate limits and temporary lockouts for repeated reset requests.
Ensure reset tokens expire quickly and are invalidated after use.
Log reset requests without storing tokens or passwords.

output_examples.input: Summarize dependency scan readiness for a Node.js service.
output: Run npm audit and record critical, high, moderate, and low counts.
Block release for any critical vulnerability or more than five high findings.
Document accepted false positives with package name, advisory, and reason.
Run fixes only after confirming dependency changes are acceptable.

output_examples.input: Check SSRF protections for a URL fetch endpoint.
output: Require a domain allowlist for outbound requests.
Block localhost, private address ranges, and cloud metadata endpoints.
Disable redirects or validate each redirect target.
Set short timeouts and isolate external fetchers from internal services.

best_practices: Ask before running scanner commands that install tools, access networks, or modify project files.
Treat secret scan reports and vulnerability evidence as confidential project data.
Pair automated scan output with manual review of authentication, authorization, and data handling.
anti_patterns: Do not mark a release safe only because one scanner returns no findings.
Do not run dependency fix commands without reviewing the resulting package changes.
Do not paste secrets, tokens, or raw sensitive scan output into public issue trackers.

faq.question: Does this skill run security scans by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides commands and workflows that an agent or user can run with appropriate approval.

faq.question: Which security standard does it follow?
faq.answer: It centers on OWASP Top 10 and adds practical checks for authentication, scanning, headers, logging, GDPR, and SOC 2.

faq.question: Can it replace a penetration test?
faq.answer: No. It helps prepare and structure reviews, but expert testing is still needed for high-risk systems.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill is defensive, but it includes local command execution, dependency fixes, network-aware scanners, and secret scan handling.

faq.question: Is it suitable for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.

faq.question: What should users review before applying recommendations?
faq.answer: Review command effects, scan false positives, dependency changes, compliance assumptions, and any output that may contain sensitive data.
